question: Define an open set in a metric space (X, d). Provide the precise mathematical definition and explain its meaning in your own words.
expectedPoints: A set U ⊆ X is open if for every point x ∈ U, there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x, ε) is entirely contained in U.
The definition relies on the concept of an open ball, B(x, ε) = { y ∈ X | d(x, y) < ε }.
The intuitive meaning is that every point in an open set is an 'interior point'; you can always move a little bit in any direction from that point and still remain inside the set.

question: Prove that the intersection of a finite number of open sets in a metric space is itself an open set.
expectedPoints: Let U₁, U₂, ..., Uₙ be a finite collection of open sets. Let x be a point in their intersection, ∩_{i=1}^n U_i.
Since each U_i is open, for each i, there exists an ε_i > 0 such that B(x, ε_i) ⊆ U_i.
Take ε = min{ ε₁, ε₂, ..., εₙ }. Since the collection is finite, ε > 0.
Then, B(x, ε) ⊆ B(x, ε_i) ⊆ U_i for every i. Therefore, B(x, ε) ⊆ ∩_{i=1}^n U_i, proving the intersection is open.

question: Let ℚ be the set of rational numbers with the standard Euclidean metric. Explain why ℚ is not an open subset of ℝ. Then, prove that ℚ, considered as a metric space itself, does have non-empty open sets and describe what they look like.
expectedPoints: ℚ is not open in ℝ because any open interval in ℝ around a rational point q ∈ ℚ contains irrational numbers. Therefore, no open ball B_ℝ(q, ε) is entirely contained in ℚ.
However, in the metric subspace (ℚ, d), the open sets are of the form U = ℚ ∩ V, where V is an open set in ℝ.
For example, the set B_ℚ(q, ε) = { r ∈ ℚ | |q - r| < ε } is an open ball and hence an open set in the metric space ℚ.
Any open set in ℚ is a union of such open intervals intersected with ℚ. They are dense and, crucially, not open when viewed as subsets of the larger space ℝ.
